EMG modelling

Johannes Zauner, Lucien Bickerstaff

2026-02-09

Setup

# Seed for reproducibility
set.seed(2025)

# Libraries
library(here)
library(tidyverse)
library(LightLogR)
library(patchwork)
library(lme4)
library(visreg)
library(mgcv)
library(sjPlot)
library(broom.mixed)
library(MuMIn)

source(here("R", "helpers.R"))

EMG data

# Import EMG activity data
emg_data <- read_csv(here("data", "emg_dose_resp_modelling", "emg_pilot_data_averages.csv"))
## Rows: 408 Columns: 7
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## dbl (7): channel, condition, melanopic_edi, photopic_lux, participant_id, em...
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
# create muscle specific response columns
data <- emg_data %>% 
  pivot_wider(names_from = channel,
              values_from = emg_response, # Here we take change counts and not % of change!
              names_prefix = "emg_response_"
              )

Visualize

# We take orbicularis oculi response (response_2)
Plot_unscaled <-
data |> 
  ggplot(aes(y = emg_response_2, x = melanopic_edi)) +
  geom_violin(aes(group = melanopic_edi), scale = "width") +
  stat_summary(aes(group = melanopic_edi), fun = mean) +
  stat_summary(aes(group = melanopic_edi), fun = median, col = "red", size = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Violin plots of response against stimulus with mean (black) and median (red) response\nblue: linear model with 0lx, green: linear model without 0lx") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  geom_smooth(aes(group = melanopic_edi == 0), col = "green4", fill = "green4",
              method = "lm")

Plot_scaled <- 
  Plot_unscaled +
  scale_x_continuous(trans = "symlog", breaks = c(0,10^(0:5))) +
  labs(title = NULL)

Plot_unscaled_log10 <-
  Plot_unscaled +
    scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = NULL, subtitle = "Y scaled log10")
  
Plot_scaled_log10 <- 
  Plot_scaled +
  scale_y_log10()

(Plot_unscaled + Plot_scaled) /
(Plot_unscaled_log10 + Plot_scaled_log10)

Model

Including random intercept for pid

data <- 
data |> 
  mutate(is.zero = melanopic_edi == 0,
         log_mel_edi = pmax(melanopic_edi, 1) |> log10())

m1_formula <- log10(emg_response_2) ~ is.zero + log_mel_edi + (1| participant_id)
m0_formula <- log10(emg_response_2) ~ log_mel_edi + (1| participant_id)
        
model1 <- lmer(m1_formula, data   = data)
model0 <- lmer(m0_formula, data   = data)

summary(model1)
## Linear mixed model fit by REML ['lmerMod']
## Formula: log10(emg_response_2) ~ is.zero + log_mel_edi + (1 | participant_id)
##    Data: data
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: -107.9
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -3.13591 -0.59801  0.03686  0.59131  2.13355 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups         Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  participant_id (Intercept) 0.02192  0.1481  
##  Residual                   0.01282  0.1132  
## Number of obs: 108, groups:  participant_id, 18
## 
## Fixed effects:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value
## (Intercept)  1.19768    0.09155   13.08
## is.zeroTRUE  0.87328    0.08875    9.84
## log_mel_edi  0.36138    0.02560   14.12
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr) i.TRUE
## is.zeroTRUE -0.882       
## log_mel_edi -0.915  0.944
summary(model0)
## Linear mixed model fit by REML ['lmerMod']
## Formula: log10(emg_response_2) ~ log_mel_edi + (1 | participant_id)
##    Data: data
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: -44.9
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2.5660 -0.6963 -0.1510  0.6951  2.3317 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups         Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  participant_id (Intercept) 0.01962  0.1401  
##  Residual                   0.02663  0.1632  
## Number of obs: 108, groups:  participant_id, 18
## 
## Fixed effects:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value
## (Intercept)  1.99198    0.04935   40.36
## log_mel_edi  0.12351    0.01216   10.16
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##             (Intr)
## log_mel_edi -0.672
performance::check_model(model1)

performance::check_model(model0)

anova(model0, model1)
## refitting model(s) with ML (instead of REML)
## Data: data
## Models:
## model0: log10(emg_response_2) ~ log_mel_edi + (1 | participant_id)
## model1: log10(emg_response_2) ~ is.zero + log_mel_edi + (1 | participant_id)
##        npar      AIC      BIC logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)    
## model0    4  -48.701  -37.972 28.350   -56.701                         
## model1    5 -113.489 -100.078 61.745  -123.489 66.788  1  3.023e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
visreg(model1, jitter = TRUE)

Not a perfect fit, but good enough diagnostics. Better than the Gamma model.

Checking whether the dependency is log linear around the data

This is done to see whether if we free the modelling approach from the linearity assumption, does it capture non-linear behaviour that we might have overlooked?

data$log_mel_shift <- log(data$melanopic_edi + 1)

m_gamm <- gam(
  emg_response_2 ~ s(log_mel_shift, k = 6) +
    s(participant_id, bs = "re"),
  family = Gamma(link = "log"),
  data = data
)

summary(m_gamm)
## 
## Family: Gamma 
## Link function: log 
## 
## Formula:
## emg_response_2 ~ s(log_mel_shift, k = 6) + s(participant_id, 
##     bs = "re")
## 
## Parametric coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  5.45776    0.04909   111.2   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Approximate significance of smooth terms:
##                         edf Ref.df     F p-value    
## s(log_mel_shift)  2.221e+00  2.559 37.69  <2e-16 ***
## s(participant_id) 1.321e-05  1.000  0.00   0.581    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## R-sq.(adj) =  0.357   Deviance explained = 55.1%
## GCV = 0.20285  Scale est. = 0.25602   n = 108
plot(m_gamm, all.terms = TRUE)

Looking good:

That is a generalized additive mixed model. It does not assume a linear relationship between the variables and thus is useful if we want to assess whether a non-linear relationship agrees with our linear model - in the relevant region, that is. So if we only look to the right of the plot - that is where the non-zero Mel EDI data are. And there we don’t see relevant non-linearities, so our approach is not missing anything important. You can see where our data are by the small lines sticking up from the x-axis

Saving final model

The model including is.zero is a better fit. We drop participant-dependent slopes as they prevent models from converging.

save_rds_safe(model1, here("outputs", "emg_lmm.rds"))

Manuscript-ready outputs

tab_model(
  model1,
  show.re.var = TRUE,   # show random-effect SD/variance
  show.icc = TRUE,      # intraclass correlation
  show.r2 = TRUE,       # pseudo-R² if available
  pred.labels = c("Intercept", "is.zeroTRUE", "log_mel_edi"),
  dv.labels = "emg_response",
  file = here("outputs", "model_table.html")
)
  emg_response
Predictors Estimates CI p
Intercept 1.20 1.02 – 1.38 <0.001
is.zeroTRUE 0.87 0.70 – 1.05 <0.001
log_mel_edi 0.36 0.31 – 0.41 <0.001
Random Effects
σ2 0.01
τ00 participant_id 0.02
ICC 0.63
N participant_id 18
Observations 108
Marginal R2 / Conditional R2 0.518 / 0.822 
coef_table <- tidy(model1, effects = "fixed", conf.int = TRUE)
coef_table
## # A tibble: 3 × 7
##   effect term        estimate std.error statistic conf.low conf.high
##   <chr>  <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
## 1 fixed  (Intercept)    1.20     0.0915     13.1     1.02      1.38 
## 2 fixed  is.zeroTRUE    0.873    0.0887      9.84    0.699     1.05 
## 3 fixed  log_mel_edi    0.361    0.0256     14.1     0.311     0.412
r2 <- r.squaredGLMM(model1)
r2
##            R2m       R2c
## [1,] 0.5177119 0.8220023
